7	(\cref{def:gr_transition}) satisfying the cocycle condition
8	(\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}), and proved that the structure morphism
9	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated})
10	and proper (\cref{lem:gr_proper}). This chapter adds the next layer: the
11	\emph{tautological rank-\(d\) quotient}
12	\(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of the
13	trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf, and the \emph{universal property} that makes
14	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) the fine moduli space of rank-\(d\) locally free quotients of
15	\(\mathcal{O}^{r}\) -- equivalently, the representing scheme of the Quot functor
16	of the trivial sheaf.
17	
18	Throughout, \(r \ge d \ge 1\) are the fixed integers of the previous chapter, and
19	for a size-\(d\) subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) we write \(R^I\) for the
20	chart ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) of \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) and \(R^I_J\) for the
21	localisation along \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) defining the overlap
22	\(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\).
23	
24	\section{The tautological quotient on the charts}
25	\label{sec:grquot_chart}
26	
27	\begin{definition}
28	[Chart quotient \(u^I\)]
29	  \label{def:gr_chart_quotient}
30	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartQuotientMap}
31	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
32	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
33	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
34	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L898-L910).
35	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
36	  % "We next define a rank $d$ locally free sheaf $\U$ on $\grass(r,d)$
37	  %  together with a surjective homomorphism $\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$.
38	  %  On each $U^I$ we define a surjective homomorphism
39	  %  $u^I :\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I} \to  \oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
40	  %  by the matrix $X^I$."
41	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
42	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). On the affine chart
43	  \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) define the
44	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}\)-linear homomorphism of trivial bundles
45	  \[
46	    u^I \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}
47	      \;\longrightarrow\; \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}
48	  \]
49	  given by left multiplication by the universal \(d \times r\) matrix \(X^I\)
50	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}): on global sections it is the \(R^I\)-linear
51	  map \((R^I)^{r} \to (R^I)^{d}\), \(v \mapsto X^I v\). Since the \(I\)-th minor
52	  \(X^I_I\) of \(X^I\) is the identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\), the composite of
53	  \(u^I\) with the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates
54	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) is the
55	  identity; hence \(u^I\) is a split surjection of locally free sheaves, with
56	  target the free rank-\(d\) sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\).
57	\end{definition}
58	
59	\begin{definition}
60	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
61	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
62	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
63	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
64	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme}
65	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
66	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
67	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
68	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
69	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
70	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
71	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
72	  %  by putting
73	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
74	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
75	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
76	  \[
77	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
78	  \]
79	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
80	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
81	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
82	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
83	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
84	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
85	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
86	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
87	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
88	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
89	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
90	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	\begin{definition}
94	[The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}^{r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\)]
95	  \label{def:tautological_quotient}
96	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient}
97	  \uses{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_chart_quotient,
98	    def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_universalMinorInv, lem:gr_cocycle}
99	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
100	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
101	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L908-L910).
102	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
103	  % "This is compatible with the homomorphisms $u^I$,
104	  %  so we get a surjective homomorphism
105	  %  $u : \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$."
106	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
107	  The chart quotients \(u^I : \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
108	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}) are compatible with the bundle gluing
109	  \((g_{I,J})\) of \(\mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}): over
110	  each overlap \(U^I_J\) one has the identity of \(d \times r\) matrices
111	  \[
112	    g_{I,J}\, X^I \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I \;=\; X^J \qquad \text{on } U^I_J,
113	  \]
114	  which is precisely the defining formula of the chart transition
115	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}).
116	  Hence the local maps \(u^I\), transported through the trivialisations of
117	  \(\mathcal{U}\), agree on overlaps and glue to a single surjective
118	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-linear homomorphism
119	  \[
120	    u \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
121	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\; \mathcal{U},
122	  \]
123	  the \emph{tautological} (or \emph{universal}) rank-\(d\) quotient of the trivial
124	  rank-\(r\) sheaf. It is surjective because each \(u^I\) is (split) surjective and
125	  surjectivity is local on \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and \(\mathcal{U}\) is locally
126	  free of rank \(d\) by \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}. The pair
127	  \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is the family of rank-\(d\) quotients carried
128	  by \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) itself.
129	\end{definition}
130	
131	\section{The functor of points}
132	\label{sec:grquot_functor}
133	
134	\begin{definition}
135	[The Grassmannian / Quot-of-trivial-sheaf functor \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\)]
136	  \label{def:grassmannian_functor}
137	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.functor}
138	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
139	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
140	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L557-L564).
141	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
142	  % "represents the
143	  %  contravariant functor
144	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
145	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
146	  %  set of all equivalence classes
147	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
148	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$."
149	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
150	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
151	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
152	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
153	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
154	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
155	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
156	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
157	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
158	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
159	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
160	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
161	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
162	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
163	  \[
164	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
165	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
166	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
167	  \]
168	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
169	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
170	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
171	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
172	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
173	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
174	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
175	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
176	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{The universal property}
180	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
181	
182	\begin{theorem}
183	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
184	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
185	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
186	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
187	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
188	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
189	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
190	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
191	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
192	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
193	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
194	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
195	  %  contravariant functor
196	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
197	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
198	  %  set of all equivalence classes
199	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
200	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
201	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
202	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
203	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
204	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
205	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
206	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
207	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
208	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
209	  \[
210	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
211	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
212	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
213	    \qquad
214	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
215	      \;\longmapsto\;
216	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
217	  \]
218	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
219	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
220	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
221	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
222	  universal family.
223	\end{theorem}
224	
225	\begin{proof}
226	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
227	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
228	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
229	    def:grassmannian_functor}
230	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
231	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
232	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
233	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
234	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
235	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
236	
237	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
238	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
239	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
240	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
241	  the composite
242	  \[
243	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
244	      \xhookrightarrow{\ \iota_I\ } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
245	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
246	  \]
247	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
248	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
249	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
250	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
251	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
252	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
253	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
254	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
255	
256	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
257	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
258	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
259	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
260	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
261	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
262	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
263	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
264	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
265	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
266	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
267	
268	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
269	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
270	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
271	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
272	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
273	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}). Hence
274	  \(\varphi_I\) and \(\varphi_J\) land in the overlap charts and agree there after
275	  the transition \(\theta_{I,J}\), so by the cocycle condition
276	  (\cref{lem:gr_cocycle}) the \(\varphi_I\) glue to a single morphism
277	  \(\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}). By
278	  construction \(\varphi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F},
279	  q \rangle\), since this holds on each \(T_I\) and the equivalence class is
280	  determined by \(\ker(q)\).
281	
282	  \emph{Uniqueness.} If \(\psi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) also satisfies
283	  \(\psi^{*}\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle = \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), then
284	  on \(\psi^{-1}(U^I)\) the pulled-back tautological quotient \(\psi^{*}u^I\)
285	  equals \(q\) in the \(I\)-trivialisation, which forces \(\psi^{*}X^I = M^I\) and
286	  hence \(\psi = \varphi_I\) there; as the \(U^I\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\),
287	  \(\psi = \varphi\). Thus \(\varphi\) is the unique morphism inducing
288	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\), and \(\varphi \mapsto \langle
289	  \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u \rangle\) is bijective.
290	
291	  Therefore \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\) is a fine
292	  moduli space: it represents \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\), so
293	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
294	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
295	\end{proof}
296

897	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
898	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
899	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
900	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
901	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
902	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
903	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
904	
905	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
906	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
907	
908	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
909	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
910	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
911	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
912	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
913	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
914	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
915	
916	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
917	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
918	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
919	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
920	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
921	  \(A_g\).
922	
923	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
924	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
925	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
926	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
927	
928	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
929	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
930	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
931	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
932	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
933	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
934	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
935	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
936	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
937	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
938	
939	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
940	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
941	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
942	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
943	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
944	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
945	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
946	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
947	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
948	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
949	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
950	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
951	
952	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
953	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
954	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
955	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
956	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
957	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
958	
959	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
960	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
961	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
962	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
963	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
964	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
965	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
966	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
967	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
968	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
969	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
970	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
971	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
972	
973	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
974	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
975	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
976	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
977	\end{proof}

994	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
995	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
996	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
997	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
998	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
999	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1000	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).

1004	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1005	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1006	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1007	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1008	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1009	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1010	\end{proof}

1255	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1256	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1257	
1258	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1259	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1260	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1261	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1262	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1263	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1264	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1265	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1266	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.

1280	[Half 1 helper — transfer of localized-module finiteness across models]
1281	  \label{lem:finite_localizedModule_transfer}
1282	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.finite_localizedModule_of_isLocalizedModule}
1283	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, \(S \subseteq R\) a multiplicative submonoid,
1284	  and \(M\) an \(R\)-module. Let \(R_S\) be an \(R\)-algebra that localizes \(R\)
1285	  at \(S\), and let \(N\) be an \(R\)-module carrying a compatible \(R_S\)-module
1286	  structure, together with an \(R\)-linear map \(\varphi : M \to N\) exhibiting
1287	  \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\) (\(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}\,S\,
1288	  \varphi\)). If \(N\) is a finite \(R_S\)-module, then the canonical localized
1289	  module \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) is a finite module over the canonical
1290	  localization \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1291	\end{lemma}
1292	
1293	\begin{proof}
1294	  The localization map \(\varphi\) provides an \(R\)-linear isomorphism
1295	  \(e : N \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\) (universal property
1296	  of localized modules), and the algebra localization provides an \(R\)-algebra
1297	  isomorphism \(\psi : R_S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathrm{Localization}\,S\). These
1298	  are compatible: \(e\) is semilinear over \(\psi\), i.e.\ \(e(a \cdot x) =
1299	  \psi(a) \cdot e(x)\) for \(a \in R_S\), \(x \in N\) (both sides are pinned by
1300	  clearing a common denominator \(s \in S\), where the \(R\)-actions agree). A
1301	  finite \(R_S\)-spanning set \(T\) of \(N\) therefore maps under \(e\) to a
1302	  finite set whose \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\)-span is all of
1303	  \(\mathrm{LocalizedModule}\,S\,M\); hence the canonical model is finite over
1304	  \(\mathrm{Localization}\,S\).
1305	\end{proof}

1351	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1a — local generators restrict along a basic-open immersion]
1352	  \label{lem:gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1353	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_localGenerators_restrict}
1354	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1355	  modules. Suppose that on an open \(Y \subseteq X\) the restriction \(\F|_Y\) carries a
1356	  finite generating family of sections, i.e. a datum exhibiting \(\F|_Y\) as globally
1357	  generated by finitely many sections (a finite \(\mathrm{LocalGeneratorsData}\) /
1358	  \(\mathrm{GeneratingSections}\) for \(\F.\mathrm{over}\,Y\)). Then for any open immersion
1359	  \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the restricted family — the images of those generators under
1360	  the section-restriction \(\Gamma(\F, Y) \to \Gamma(\F, D(g))\) — is again a finite
1361	  generating family of \(\F|_{D(g)}\). Generation survives passage to the smaller open
1362	  (a family that generates every stalk over \(Y\) generates every stalk over \(D(g)\)),
1363	  and the index set, and hence finiteness, is unchanged.
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{proof}
1367	  A family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_Y\) precisely when the
1368	  induced map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) is surjective on every stalk
1369	  \(\F_y\,(y \in Y)\). Restricting along the open immersion \(D(g) \hookrightarrow Y\) the
1370	  generators map to \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\), and for a point \(x \in D(g)\) the stalk
1371	  \(\F_x\) and the comparison map agree with the value at \(x\) computed over \(Y\), since
1372	  stalks are insensitive to shrinking the ambient open. Hence the induced map
1373	  \(\OO_{D(g)}^{\oplus I} \to \F|_{D(g)}\) is surjective on every stalk over \(D(g)\), so
1374	  \(\{s_i|_{D(g)}\}_{i \in I}\) generates \(\F|_{D(g)}\). The index set \(I\) is the same
1375	  finite set, so finiteness is preserved.
1376	\end{proof}

1378	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1b — finite standard-basic-open subcover of an affine open]
1379	  \label{lem:gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_affine_finite_standard_subcover}
1381	  Let \(W\) be an affine open of a scheme \(X\), with the canonical identification
1382	  \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\). Given an arbitrary open cover \(\{U_\alpha\}\) of \(W\),
1383	  there is a finite family \(t \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\) with
1384	  \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\) such that each basic open \(D(g)\) (\(g \in t\)) is
1385	  contained in some cover member \(U_\alpha\). In particular the \(D(g)\,(g \in t)\) form a
1386	  finite standard-basic-open subcover of \(W\) refining \(\{U_\alpha\}\).
1387	\end{lemma}
1388	
1389	\begin{proof}
1390	  Under \(W \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) the basic opens \(D(g)\,(g \in \Gamma(X, W))\) form a
1391	  basis of the topology (\(\mathrm{PrimeSpectrum.isBasis\_basic\_opens}\)). Hence each
1392	  cover member \(U_\alpha\) is a union of basic opens, and the collection of all basic
1393	  opens \(D(g)\) that are contained in some \(U_\alpha\) still covers \(W\). The affine
1394	  scheme \(W\) is quasi-compact, so finitely many of these basic opens
1395	  \(D(g_1), \dots, D(g_n)\) already cover \(W\); set
1396	  \(t = \{g_1, \dots, g_n\} \subseteq \Gamma(X, W)\), a finite family, each \(D(g_i)\)
1397	  contained in some \(U_\alpha\) by construction. That the \(D(g_i)\) cover
1398	  \(W = \Spec \Gamma(X, W)\) — i.e. no prime ideal contains all the \(g_i\) — is exactly
1399	  the condition \(\mathrm{Ideal.span}\,t = \top\).
1400	\end{proof}

1402	\begin{lemma}[Half 2, seam 1c — finite generation equals a finite free epimorphism]
1403	  \label{lem:gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1404	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_finite_gen_iff_free_epi}
1405	  Let \(Y\) be an open of a scheme and \(\F\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules. Then
1406	  \(\F|_Y\) is globally generated by a finite family of sections if and only if there is a
1407	  finite index set \(I\) and an epimorphism of \(\OO_Y\)-modules
1408	  \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\).
1409	\end{lemma}
1410	
1411	\begin{proof}
1412	  A finite family of sections \(\{s_i\}_{i \in I}\) of \(\F|_Y\) is the same datum as an
1413	  \(\OO_Y\)-linear map \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \to \F|_Y\) sending the \(i\)-th basis section to
1414	  \(s_i\). The family generates \(\F|_Y\) (the induced map is surjective on every stalk)
1415	  exactly when this map is an epimorphism of sheaves of modules. Thus a finite generating
1416	  family yields a finite-free epimorphism, and conversely the images of the basis sections
1417	  of any epimorphism \(\OO_Y^{\oplus I} \twoheadrightarrow \F|_Y\) with \(I\) finite form a
1418	  finite generating family.
1419	\end{proof}

1487	  \label{lem:gf_affine_qcoh_Gamma_epi}

1491	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB, proof
1492	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2106-L2107) — the implication
1493	  % "$\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ generated by finitely many sections $\Rightarrow$
1494	  % $M_{f_i}$ finitely generated" is exactly section-level surjectivity of a sheaf
1495	  % epi over an affine.
1496	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1497	  % "such that $\widetilde M|_{D(f_i)}$ is generated by finitely many sections.
1498	  %  Thus $M_{f_i}$ is finitely generated for each $i$."
1499	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB (sections of a
1500	  generated sheaf over an affine).}
1501	  Let \(V\) be an affine open of a scheme, with \(B = \Gamma(X, V)\) and
1502	  \(V \cong \Spec B\), and let \(\pi : \mathcal{G} \to \F\) be an epimorphism of
1503	  quasi-coherent sheaves of modules over \(V\). Then the induced map on sections
1504	  \(\Gamma(\pi) : \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to \Gamma(\F, V)\) is a surjective
1505	  \(B\)-module map.
1506	\end{lemma}
1507	
1508	\begin{proof}
1509	  \uses{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization, lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib,
1510	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}
1511	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 01PB (sections of a generated sheaf over an
1512	  affine).}
1513	  Write \(V \cong \Spec B\) and let \(\Gamma\) be the affine section functor on
1514	  \((\Spec B).\mathrm{Modules}\), so that \(\Gamma(\pi) = \Gamma(\mathcal{G}, V) \to
1515	  \Gamma(\F, V)\) is the map in question. By \cref{lem:tilde_adjunction_mathlib} the
1516	  associated-sheaf functor \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is left adjoint to \(\Gamma\), with counit the
1517	  natural transformation whose component at a sheaf \(\mathcal{M}\) is
1518	  \(\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ} : \widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{M}, V)} \to \mathcal{M}\).
1519	
1520	  Because \(\mathcal{G}\) and \(\F\) are quasi-coherent, the qcoh-sections descent
1521	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}, fed through the counit-iso assembly
1522	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_isLocalizedModule_restrict}) makes both counit components
1523	  \(\mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) and \(\F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) isomorphisms. Naturality of the
1524	  counit on \(\pi\) gives the commuting square
1525	  \[
1526	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)} \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1}
1527	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi,
1528	    \qquad\text{equivalently}\qquad
1529	    \widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}
1530	      = \F.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}^{-1} \circ \pi \circ \mathcal{G}.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}.
1531	  \]
1532	  The right-hand side is a composite of an isomorphism, the epimorphism \(\pi\), and an
1533	  isomorphism, hence is itself an epimorphism. Thus \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\pi)}\) is an epimorphism
1534	  of sheaves of modules. Since the left adjoint \(\widetilde{(-)}\) is faithful, it reflects
1535	  epimorphisms; therefore \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is an epimorphism in \(\mathrm{Mod}\,B\), i.e. the
1536	  \(B\)-linear map \(\Gamma(\pi)\) is surjective.
1537	
1538	  This is the structural content of the vanishing of higher quasi-coherent cohomology on an
1539	  affine — the failure of \(\Gamma\) to be right exact would obstruct exactly this surjectivity —
1540	  obtained here directly from the affine adjunction without invoking a cohomology computation.
1541	\end{proof}
